coredata.dc:title: Exact roles of individual chemical forms of nitrogen in the photoluminescent properties of nitrogen-doped carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Sudolská, Mária
Otyepka, Michal
coredata.dc:description: The chemical and structural complexity of doped carbon dots impedes the understanding of their photoluminescence and hence rational design of doped carbon dots with tailored properties. We present an ensemble approach for modeling the absorption and fluorescence spectra of nitrogen-doped carbon dots that reveals how the type and content of nitrogen, as well as chromophore variability, affect the optical properties. We combined time-dependent density functional theory calculations and Boltzmann averaging of distributions of doped carbon dots models containing nitrogen in the form of amine, pyridinic, and pyrrolic moieties. The method was shown to be robust, provided reliable spectra and has applications in the systematic exploration of doped carbon dot optical properties. Amine groups caused red-shifts in the absorption and fluorescence maxima of nitrogen-doped carbon dots, while pyridinic and pyrrolic doping had the opposite effect, shown by blue-shifts in the absorption and fluorescence maxima.

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authlast highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: One-layer chromophores represented by the lowest electronic energy conformers within each class containing –NH2 (P-1NH2 and P-2NH2), pyridinic (P-1pd-N and P-2pd-N), and pyrrolic nitrogen (P-1pl-N and P-2pl-N). Green, blue, and grey represent carbon, nitrogen, and hydrogen atoms, respectively.

Two-layer chromophores represented by the lowest electronic energy conformers within each class (2P-1NH2-1, 2P-2NH2-1, 2P-1pd-N-1, 2P-2pd-N-2, 2P-1pl-N-1, and 2P-2pl-N-1). Green, blue, and grey represent carbon, nitrogen, and hydrogen atoms, respectively.

Calculated absorption and fluorescence (insets) spectra of pyrene chromophores with single –NH2 substitutions (full lines), their rescaled counterparts (dashed lines) and the final averaged spectrum (thick black line), in a vacuum (a) and water (b). P-1NH2-1, P-1NH2-2 and P-1NH2-3 are indicated by blue, green, and red lines, respectively. The absorption and fluorescence maxima wavelengths are given in nm.

Calculated electron density differences between the ground and selected excited states in P-1NH2-1 (a), P-1pd-N-1 (b) and P-1pl-N-1 (c). Red/blue indicates charge accumulation/depletion. Wavelengths (in nm) and oscillator strength values are listed below each transition.

Calculated absorption and fluorescence (insets) spectra of pyrene chromophores with double –NH2 substitutions (full lines), their rescaled counterparts (dashed lines) and the final averaged spectrum (thick black line), in a vacuum (a) and water (b).

Calculated electron density differences between the ground and selected excited states in P-2NH2-1 (a), P-2pd-N-1 (b) and P-2pl-N-1 (c). Red/blue indicates charge accumulation/depletion. Wavelengths (in nm) and oscillator strength values are listed below each transition.

Calculated absorption and fluorescence (insets) spectra of pyridinic nitrogen-doped structures: P-1pd-N systems in a vacuum (a) and water (b), and P-2pd-N systems in a vacuum (c) and water (d). The spectra of the most stable structures in each class are shown as colored full lines, their rescaled counterparts as dashed lines, and the final averaged spectra are represented by thick black lines. In case of P-2pd-N in water, only the absorption and fluorescence for P-2pd-N-1 is shown.

Calculated absorption and fluorescence (insets) spectra of pyrrolic nitrogen-doped structures: P-1pl-N systems in a vacuum (a) and water (b), and P-2pl-N systems in a vacuum (c) and water (d). The spectra of the most stable structures in each class are shown as colored full lines, their rescaled counterparts as dashed lines, and the final averaged spectra are represented by thick black lines. In case of P-1pl-N in water, only the absorption and fluorescence for P-1pl-N-1 is shown.

Absorption spectra of the samples 2P-1NH2 (a), 2P-2NH2 (b), 2P-1pd-N (c), 2P-2pd-N (d), 2P-1pl-N (e) and 2P-2pl-N (f). The spectra of the individual conformers in each class are shown as full colored lines, their rescaled counterparts as dashed lines, and the final averaged spectra are represented by thick black lines.

(a) Ensemble absorption spectra of the one-layer systems P-1NH2 (orange), P-2NH2 (red), P-1pd-N (light blue), P-2pd-N (blue), P-1pl-N (light green), and P-2pl-N (green) in a vacuum compared to the absorption spectrum of pyrene (black). Insets: fluorescence spectra of the one-layer systems in a vacuum (full line) and water (dashed line). (b) Ensemble absorption spectra of the two-layer systems 2P-1NH2 (orange), 2P-2NH2 (red), 2P-1pd-N (light blue), 2P-2pd-N (blue), 2P-1pl-N (light green), and 2P-2pl-N (green). The absorption spectra of the respective one-layer subunits functionalized/doped with one and two nitrogen moieties are shown in grey and black, respectively. Red/blue arrows indicate red-/blue-shifts in the absorption and fluorescence.
Number of structures (isomers/conformers) a within each class of the N-CD chromophore models, and their carbon and nitrogen contents (wt%).

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The numbers of all possible isomers and selected studied conformers are given for 1L and 2L structures, respectively.

head.ce:title: Exact roles of individual chemical forms of nitrogen in the photoluminescent properties of nitrogen-doped carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Regional Centre of Advanced Technologies and Materials, Department of Physical Chemistry, Palacký University Olomouc, tř. 17. Listopadu 12, 771 46 Olomouc, Czech Republic

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Regional Centre of Advanced Technologies and Materials, Department of Physical Chemistry, Palacký University Olomouc

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The chemical and structural complexity of doped carbon dots impedes the understanding of their photoluminescence and hence rational design of doped carbon dots with tailored properties. We present an ensemble approach for modeling the absorption and fluorescence spectra of nitrogen-doped carbon dots that reveals how the type and content of nitrogen, as well as chromophore variability, affect the optical properties. We combined time-dependent density functional theory calculations and Boltzmann averaging of distributions of doped carbon dots models containing nitrogen in the form of amine, pyridinic, and pyrrolic moieties. The method was shown to be robust, provided reliable spectra and has applications in the systematic exploration of doped carbon dot optical properties. Amine groups caused red-shifts in the absorption and fluorescence maxima of nitrogen-doped carbon dots, while pyridinic and pyrrolic doping had the opposite effect, shown by blue-shifts in the absorption and fluorescence maxima.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) undoubtedly represent the most rapidly developing class of carbon nanoallotropes, and have a wide range of applications [1–5]. The optical features of CDs can be significantly improved by nitrogen-doping, as N-doped CDs (N-CDs) exhibit enhanced UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence properties. This, together with the low toxicity, biocompatibility, and environmentally friendly nature of N-CDs, makes them viable candidates for various applications, including biological imaging and drug delivery, photocatalytic reduction and oxidation reactions of metals and organic compounds, dye-sensitized and organic solar cells, light-emitting devices, supercapacitors, as well as pH and chemical sensors for various organic (DNA, glucose) and inorganic species (metal ions) [6–8]. The type and amount of nitrogen centers, incorporated either directly within the carbon lattice or at the N-CD surface, can modify the absorption and emission, which can span from ultraviolet to near infrared on the electromagnetic spectrum, and improve the quantum yields when compared to nitrogen-free CDs [3,9]. The chemical nature of nitrogen in N-CDs depends on which synthetic method was utilized. For example, aromatic pyridinic, pyrrolic, and graphitic nitrogen atoms are the most abundant nitrogen species that substitute for carbon atoms in the core of N-CDs prepared from carbon and nitrogen molecular precursors via hydrothermal/solvothermal- or microwave-assisted synthesis [10–16]. On the other hand, surface nitrogen functionalities, such as amine and amide groups, can be effectively introduced through the post-synthesis treatment of oxygen containing CDs [17,18]. Various nitrogen types and content levels lead to differences in the absorption and emission properties of N-CDs. For example, red-shifted absorption and fluorescence has been attributed to the presence of surface amine groups that are capable of modulating the LUMO-HOMO gap of the aromatic carbon nanodomains within a carbon dot [17]. There is evidence that the pyridinic nitrogen is responsible for the blue-shift in the light absorption of N-CDs [19]. The design of more efficient N-CDs is preceded by an understanding of the precise roles of individual forms of nitrogen and how they impact absorption/emission spectra and quantum yield. However, the as-prepared N-CDs usually contain more than one type of nitrogen doping and/or functionalization, which makes it difficult to identify the individual nitrogen contributions to optical properties by experimental techniques alone.

Theoretical calculations have provided valuable insights into the photophysical properties of metallic and semiconductor quantum dots [20–24], in this respect, the properties of CDs and doped CDs are significantly underexplored. So far, most theoretical research has modeled CDs as one-layered systems based on polyaromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs) of various sizes and the optical properties have been addressed by either calculating LUMO-HOMO gaps through semi-empirical and density functional theory (DFT) methods or directly by vertical electronic transitions, mainly evaluated at time-dependent DFT (TD-DFT) level [3]. In addition to the studies of amorphous and graphene CDs of various shapes and sizes that contain strictly carbon and hydrogen atoms [25–29], the effects of the type and content of oxygen, nitrogen, and sulfur surface functionalization and/or doping have been assessed through the modeling of substituted and/or doped PAH based systems [30–34]. While all of the factors mentioned above have an appreciable role in the electronic structure and hence, the modulation of optical properties, the stacking of single layer structures to build two and three layer models of oxygen and nitrogen CDs have only led to mild shifting effects in the final absorption spectra [35,36]. Contrary to UV–Vis absorption, the fluorescence properties of CDs, including those that contain heteroatoms in their structure, have been rather scarcely addressed [27,31,37], and the fluorescence properties of N-CDs have not been extensively analyzed through computational studies. Clearly, the state-of-the-art knowledge base that links particular structural patterns representative of CD chromophores to their properties, such as UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence, lacks the degree of systematism that would enable the efficient design of N-CDs for specific applications.

Motivated by this deficiency, we addressed the UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence properties of N-CDs by modeling the ensembles of one- and two-layered nitrogen-doped (pyridinic and pyrrolic nitrogen) and functionalized (amine nitrogen) pyrene-based models (C16H10), which represent the aromatic (sp2) carbon domains within N-CDs [38]. Despite the small model proportions corresponding to the lower bound limit of N-CD size, restriction to the uniform pyrene-like sp2 core ruled out the effects of the size, shape and edge structure of CDs, and enabled us to concentrate solely on the effects of nitrogen doping. Moreover, the modulation of optical properties due to variability of active centers (i.e., chromophores) within an ensemble arising from different isomer/conformer formation during a common N-CD synthesis was taken into account via a procedure based on high throughput sampling of relevant structures and Boltzmann distribution. This approach allowed us to consider the diverse array of active centers that arise from the synthesis of N-CDs.

The N-CD models were based on one and two-layered pyrene structures, which contained various chemical forms of nitrogen as dopant element. The used system is rather small corresponding to the lower bond of N-CDs diameter ranging from 1 to 5nm. Similar models were successfully used in explaining optical properties of graphene CDs and oxygen functionalized CDs [38,39]. The main advantage of the model system was that it enabled focus solely on the effects of nitrogen doping and systematic exploration of all possible isomers (see below). The effects of amine (–NH2), pyridinic, and pyrrolic nitrogen types, i.e. the most abundant nitrogen types found in recently synthesized N-CDs (see, e.g., Refs. [12,13,16,40,41]), have been considered to model the nitrogen-related absorption and fluorescence of N-CDs. For each nitrogen type, one-layered (1L) models were generated by functionalization/doping a single pyrene molecule with one and two –NH2 groups, pyridinic dopant atoms, and pyrrolic rings, respectively, yielding six different classes of chromophores denoted P-1NH2, P-2NH2, P-1pd-N, P-2pd-N, P-1pl-N and P-2pl-N (Fig. 1 ). All the possible isomers within each class (Table 1 and Tables S1–S3) were considered to mimic the chromophore diversity arising from the carbonization process, which involves a manifold of reactions such as dehydrogenation, condensation, and isomerization. The processes that occur during N-CD synthesis were assumed to be thermodynamically driven, and in this way, the amount of a particular isomer i could be given by its Boltzmann population P 0,i :
P
0 , i
=
e
−
G i
R T
∑ i
e
−
G i
R T
where G
i is the Gibbs free energy of isomer i calculated at 298K and 1atm. Finally, to account for the effects of sample heterogeneity on the overall absorption and fluorescence of the whole sample consisting of various isomer structures (here, a “sample” contains all isomers belonging to the same class, cf. Fig. 1), the final absorption and fluorescence spectra for each chromophore class were constructed as the sum of the individual isomers’ spectra scaled by their Boltzmann populations. That is, the oscillator strength values of electronic transitions in a particular isomer i were multiplied (weighted) by the respective Boltzmann population P 0,i to obtain the scaled spectrum of isomer i mirroring its abundance in a sample. Thus, for each structure, the computed oscillator strength values were multiplied by their corresponding Boltzmann weights, and the scaled line spectra were then convoluted by a Gaussian function assuming broadening of peaks with σ=10nm. Next, the scaled convoluted spectra of the individual isomers within a class were summed to generate the final absorption spectrum of a class. The same approach has been utilized in the calculations of the sample fluorescence spectra. First, the S1 →S0 deexcitation wavelengths and oscillator strengths for isomers within a class were calculated (see below). Second, the oscillator strength values were scaled with the respective Boltzmann abundances and convoluted to obtain the fluorescence spectral shapes. Finally, the scaled convoluted spectra were combined into the fluorescence spectrum of the whole sample.

The most stable one-layered isomers within each class (i.e., P-1NH2-1, P-2NH2-1, P-1pd-N-1, P-1pd-N-2, P-1pl-N-1 and P-2pl-N-1) were used to obtain the six new classes (types) of two-layered (2L) systems (Fig. 2 ). All the 2L systems were constructed by stacking the two functionalized/doped pyrene molecules of the same type, e.g., we used two molecules of P-1NH2-1 to generate a new class consisting of two-layered systems denoted 2P-1NH2. To obtain the most probable conformers, two types of rigid scans of potential energy surface (PES) for all classes were performed: (1) the top layer was slid along the bottom layer in two directions, and (2) the top layer was rotated around an axis crossing its center of mass and perpendicular to the both one-layers. For all of the scans, we held the interlayer distance constant 3.5Å. Based on these PES scans, the lowest energy conformers were chosen (Fig. S1, S2) to represent the respective 2L chromophore types, i.e., the geometries of these selected conformers were fully optimized and used in a detailed study of UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence. To construct the final sample spectra for each 2L class, we used the procedure for the one-layer systems described above.

The geometry optimizations of the ground states (S0) and frequency analyses (performed to confirm the nature of local minima) of all 1L and 2L systems within each class were conducted using density functional theory (DFT) with the range-separated hybrid ωB97xD exchange-correlation functional [42] and the 6-31++G(d,p) Pople basis set [43,44]. The UV–Vis absorption spectra of the 1L systems were obtained within the time-dependent DFT framework using the same functional as in the S0 optimizations, and 6-31++G(d,p) basis set. For the 2L systems, 6-31+G(d) basis set was used to reduce the computational demands, since the difference across these two basis sets was negligible (cf. Fig. S3). The study of emission properties were conducted only for the 1L systems, where we assumed that Kasha's rule [45] holds, i.e., the fluorescence occurs from the lowest energy singlet excited state (S1). The S1 geometries were optimized at the ωB97xD/6-31++G(d,p) level of theory to obtain fluorescence wavelengths and intensities.

The calculations of the 1L systems were performed both in a vacuum and water. In the latter, the conductor-like polarizable continuum model (C-PCM) [46,47] was used. The water environment was considered since synthesized N-CDs are usually dispersed in water, and to reveal the effects of a polar solvent on the shifts in their absorption and emission spectra. For the 2L systems, only vacuum environment has been considered. Throughout the entire work, the Gaussian 09.D01 software package [48] was used.

For each nitrogen type (amine, pyridinic and pyrrolic), one-layer (1L) N-CD models were built by functionalization/doping with one and two –NH2 groups, pyridinic dopant atoms, and pyrrolic rings, respectively, yielding six different classes of chromophores: P-1NH2, P-2NH2, P-1pd-N, P-2pd-N, P-1pl-N, and P-2pl-N (Fig. 1). The Boltzmann populations of all the possible isomers within each class (Tables S1–S3) were calculated to mimic the chromophore diversity arising from the synthesis of CDs, and the procedure of averaging over the isomer populations and individual isomer absorption/fluorescence spectra was applied to derive the final ensemble spectra (cf. Theoretical Methods).

Six classes of the two-layer (2L) models, denoted 2P-1NH2, 2P-2NH2, 2P-1pd-N, 2P-2pd-N, 2P-1pl-N, and 2P-2pl-N (Fig. 2), were constructed by stacking the two functionalized/doped molecules of the same type – the most favorable one-layer structures were used. In the latter, only the most probable conformers were selected (Tables S4–S9). The number of considered isomers/conformers, and the content of carbon and nitrogen for the one- and two-layer models are summarized in Table 1.

The P-1NH2 chromophore class consists of a total of three isomers (Table S1). The most stable structure, P-1NH2-1, is functionalized in the position 2, and has the most favorable orientation of amine hydrogen atoms with respect to hydrogen atoms at the edges of pyrene. The other two isomers P-1NH2-2 and P-1NH2-3 are 0.4 and 0.7kcal/mol, respectively, higher in energy in a vacuum. However, in water, the relative energies of P-1NH2-2 and P-1NH2-3 with respect to P-1NH2-1 are 0.7 and 0.8kcal/mol, respectively.

The vertical absorption spectra of the singly –NH2 functionalized pyrenes (Fig. 3 ) show clear dependence on molecular structure. The highest wavelength absorption bands of individual isomers, encompassing two electronic transitions (S0 →S1 and S0 →S2) mostly of mixed π/n→π* character (Fig. 4a, Fig. S4), were centered at ∼310–330nm in a vacuum and ∼315–340nm in water. Their intensities increased slightly following hydration, i.e. the oscillator strengths (f-values) were in range 0.03–0.34 in a vacuum and 0.05–0.52 in water. In P-1NH2-1 (2-aminopyrene), the maximum absorption shifted from 231 to 240nm upon hydration, and was also accompanied by an increase in intensity, as the f-values were 1.11 and 1.55 in a vacuum and water, respectively. However, the bands of both P-1NH2-2 (4-aminopyrene) and P-1NH2-3 (1-aminopyrene) consisted of a main peak at ∼220–225nm and a shoulder peak at ∼250–260nm in both a vacuum and water. Importantly, changing the position of the –NH2 group results in a ∼20nm (∼25nm) red-shift of the low-energy band in a vacuum (water), as well as a modulation of the overall spectral shape. The averaged absorption spectra (thick black lines, Fig. 3), calculated with respect to the individual isomer populations, mirror the observations mentioned above. First, the low energy bands ranged between ∼290–350 and ∼290–360nm in a vacuum and water, respectively. Second, the absorption at wavelengths lower than 300nm in both a vacuum and water featured a single band similar to that of the most favored structure, P-1NH2-1, with minor contributions from P-1NH2-2 and P-1NH2-3.

Further we analyzed fluorescence of N-CD models containing one amine group. The fluorescence wavelengths (S1 →S0) of P-1NH2-1, P-1NH2-2, and P-1NH2-3 in a vacuum were 360, 381, and 376nm, respectively (Fig. 3a, inset, and Table S10). Fluorescence in water was appreciably red-shifted compared to the vacuum, as the S1 →S0 transitions occurred at 375, 424, and 430nm in P-1NH2-1, P-1NH2-2, and P-1NH2-3, respectively (Fig. 3b, inset, and Table S11). We note here that the fluorescence band of P-1NH2-3 (1-aminopyrene), centered at 430nm, is in agreement with the experimentally measured emission maximum of 438nm in water [49], indicating that the adopted calculations may provide reliable fluorescence maxima for the model systems used in this work. The average fluorescence of the three isomers peaked at 378nm in a vacuum and is, contrary to absorption, closer to the de-excitation wavelengths of P-1NH2-2 and P-1NH2-3. Thus, the role of the most abundant isomer, P-1NH2-1, was effectively diminished by the prevailing emission contributions from the other two isomers, P-1NH2-2 and P-1NH2-3. Similarly, the fluorescence maximum in water occurred at 427nm, which is close to the emission wavelengths of the two less stable isomers. However, the second well-resolved fluorescence band at 375nm arose as a consequence of the greater separation of emission wavelengths of the three isomers, and the elevated S0 →S1 absorption intensity of P-1NH2-1 in water. Contrary to the vacuum, the footprints of all three aminopyrene species can be distinguished from the fluorescence spectrum in water.

Unlike singly-NH2 substituted pyrenes, doubly-NH2 substituted pyrenes showed different ordering of particular isomers in a vacuum and water, respectively (Table S1). For example, amine functionalization in the positions 2 and 5 (P-2NH2-1) resulted in the most favorable isomer in a vacuum, while positions 2 and 7 (P-2NH2-4) were preferred in water. The energy differences among the P-2NH2 isomers were small; 17 (13) out of the total 29 isomers (including conformers resulting from the rotation of the –NH2 group about the C–N bond) differed from the most stable isomer, P-2NH2-1 (P-2NH2-4), by less than 1kcal/mol in the vacuum (water). Consequently, the lowest energy isomers P-2NH2-1 (in a vacuum) and P-2NH2-4 (in water) were not populated predominantly, and the contributions of isomers into the overall spectrum were more visible.

In the vacuum, the absorption (Fig. 5a) of the P-2NH2 systems above 300nm consisted of two vertical electronic transitions, at ∼325–355 and ∼310–340nm. The maximum intensity transitions (above 200nm) were in the range of ∼210–235nm. The electron density difference pictures of the most energetically favored structures (Fig. 6 a and Fig. S5) show that most of the characteristic excitations mentioned above involve –NH2 groups. The final absorption spectrum featured two bands, centered at 333 and 233nm (Fig. 5a). The averaged fluorescence wavelength was 381nm (Fig. 5a, inset). Hence, the addition of a second –NH2 group led to red-shifts in both absorption and fluorescence. This finding is in agreement with previous study stating that increased number of amine groups reduces HOMO-LUMO gap [17,18]. Similar to P-1NH2, hydration plays a crucial role in the fluorescence spectrum (Fig. 5b, inset, see also Tables S10 and S11), i.e., the ensemble emission wavelength is red-shifted by ∼50nm with respect to vacuum. On the other hand, absorption maxima were red-shifted by only ∼10nm upon hydration.

The majority of the low-end energy (S0 →S1 and S0 →S2) and high intensity (< 250nm) electronic transitions in the most populated P-1pd-N and P-2pd-N systems were of π→π* character; the only exception was the S0 →S2 excitation in P-2pd-N-1 with significant electron density contributions from the lone pairs located at nitrogen atoms (Fig. 4b and 6b, and Fig. S6). Electron density difference pictures of the significant transitions in P-1pl-N-1, P-2pl-N-1 and P-2pl-N-2 show that these are of π→π* character, and most of them involve pyrrolic rings (Fig. 4c and 6c, and Fig. S7).

Fluorescence wavelengths of P-2pl-N and P-2pd-N were blue-shifted by 44nm (87nm) and 55nm (59nm), respectively, with respect to P-2NH2 in a vacuum (water). In a vacuum, the fluorescence peaks of P-1pl-N (347nm) and P-1pd-N (342nm) were blue-shifted by 31 and 36nm with respect to P-1NH2 (378nm), respectively. In water, P-1NH2, P-1pl-N, and P-1pd-N emit at 427, 375, 383, and 383nm, respectively. Contrary to the substituent-related red-shifts observed in the –NH2 models, an increase in the number of pyridinic nitrogen and pyrrolic rings results in blue-shifts in both the absorption and emission spectra.

Similar to P-1NH2, the populations of the P-1pd-N systems in a vacuum were 68.2%, 27.7%, and 4.1% (Table S2), a distribution which was mirrored in the final absorption spectrum (Fig. 7a,b). However, the fluorescence spectrum contained only the contributions of P-1pd-N-1 and P-1pd-N-2 as a consequence of their intense S0 →S1 transitions. For the P-2pd-N and P-1pl-N systems, the spectra from the lowest energy isomers’ populations (> 95%, Tables S2 and S3) matched perfectly with the averaged spectra (Fig. 7c,d and Fig. 8a,b). Contrarily, the ensemble absorption and emission spectra of P-2pl-N were dictated by the two low energy isomers, P-2pl-N-1 and P-2pl-N-2, with populations of 62.1% and 37.2% in a vacuum, respectively (Table S3 and Fig. 8c,d). To sum up, the ensemble absorption and emission spectra are controlled by two factors: (1) the abundance of particular isomers in an ensemble given by their relative stabilities, and (2) the intensity of electronic transitions in these isomers (especially S1 →S0 for fluorescence). The analysis of 1L models clearly showed that the chemical form of nitrogen doping affected the N-CDs photoluminescent properties. The presence of amine groups led to red-shifted fluorescence, while the presence of pyridinic and pyrrolic nitrogens had the opposite effect. The magnitude of this effect red/blue-shifting effects depended on the nitrogen content in the N-CDs models.

Despite the fact that previous studies indicated that addition of more layers to one-layer CD models did not affect the addressed optical properties significantly [35,36], we constructed N-CD models including two layers in order to explicitly analyze impact of the second layer on the absorption and emission. The amine, pyridinic and pyrrolic nitrogen containing double layer models were constructed from the single layers considering the most stable isomers within each class. Since the double layer models can be arranged in various conformations because both layers may slide and rotate with respect to each other, we employed computational techniques capable to search the conformational space (cf. Theoretical Methods, and Supplementary material).

The searching procedure identified six and four plausible conformers for two-layered structures within the classes 2P-1NH2 and 2P-2NH2, respectively (Tables S4 and S5). While the structures with singly-NH2 substituted layers were of a similar stability, the Gibbs free energy difference between the most stable conformer 2P-2NH2-1 and the least stable conformer 2P-2NH2-4 was nearly 4kcal/mol. System 2P-1NH2-1 possessed graphitic-like AB stacking structure where the distance between both layers amounted to 3.4Å and two amine groups were above each other making an inter-layer N-H…N hydrogen bond interaction. Graphitic AB stacking appeared also in 2P-1NH2-4 and 2P-1NH2-5 without any apparent hydrogen bond between individual amine groups. In other systems, one layer is rotated with respect to the other one. Similarly, the energetically favorable conformers of the 2P-2NH2 system, i.e., 2P-2NH2-1, 2P-2NH2-2 and 2P-2NH2-3, had AB stacking, and interlayer amine-amine interaction helped in further stabilization of 2P-2NH2-1 and 2P-2NH2-2 conformers.

The ensemble absorption spectrum of 2P-1NH2 (Fig. 9a) featured a strong high-energy band and a weaker low-energy band with maxima at 228 and 312nm, respectively, which were nearly identical to the absorption maxima of monolayer subunit P-1NH2-1 located at 232 and 309nm. Moreover, the absorption maxima of the bands of individual 2P-1NH2 conformers lay only within 6nm (i.e., the maxima span from 226 to 232nm) and 8nm (from 308 to 316nm). Thus, the formation of a bilayer structure does not alter the overall spectrum significantly. Similarly, the ensemble absorption of 2P-2NH2 (Fig. 9b) involved two bands at 227 and 327nm, and the maxima of individual conformers fell within 226–236 and 322–328nm. These two bands, as well as a shoulder at ca. 270nm, were observed also in one-layer subunit P-2NH2-1 where the high-energy absorption peaks at 233nm and the low-energy absorption occurred at 321nm (Fig. 5a). Thus, a small red-shift (blue-shift) of 6nm was observed in the low-energy (high-energy) part of the bilayer spectrum with respect to the monolayer spectrum. Finally, the low-energy absorption intensity and band-width did not change significantly upon stacking in the both samples 2P-1NH2 and 2P-2NH2. On the other hand, absorption below 250nm was intensified upon stacking, and the spectral shapes (< 300nm) were also slightly modulated in case of 2P-2NH2. We may conclude that the absorption spectra of the two-layered systems preserve the characteristics of their individual components in accord with the literature data [35,36]. It implies that the absorption spectra of layered CDs and molecular cocktails modeling layered CDs are governed by the mixture composition, i.e., the abundance of individual components irrespective of how the components are stacked in individual CDs.

For a detailed analysis of electronic transitions, we selected representative conformers 2P-1NH2-1, 2P-1NH2-5, 2P-2NH2-1 and 2P-2NH2-3 (Fig. S8–S10). The low-end energy absorption (i.e., above 300nm) consisted of four transitions related to the transitions in the respective one-layer subunits. For example, transition S0 →S1 (330nm, f =0.033) in P-1NH2-1 (Fig. 4a) gave rise to transitions S0 →S1 (340nm, f =0.006) and S0 →S2 (328nm, f =0.034) in the 2P-1NH2-1 system (Fig. S8). In this particular system, both transitions were localized mainly in one sublayer, i.e., the first transition in the top 2-aminopyrene and the second one in the bottom one, and the contribution from the other sublayer was rather weak. The S0 →S2 preserved the oscillator strength of the original (S0 →S1 of one-layer system) transition and S0 →S1 was significantly weaker. On the contrary, transitions to S3 and S4 in 2P-1NH2-1 (Fig. S8) spanned over both subunits, and could be viewed as transitions with a partial charge-transfer (CT) character. Obviously, the effect of stacking was more significant for this high-energy transitions. The main features of S3 and S4, i.e., energy/wavelength modulation and intensity, followed the above mentioned lower energy S1 and S2 excitations. S0 →S3 was red-shifted by more than 10nm with respect to the original S0 →S2 of one-layer system (308nm, f =0.268), and was significantly less intense than S0 →S4. The transitions involved in the maximum absorption at 200–250nm of 2P-1NH2-1 were of mixed π/n→π* character, and spanned over the whole stacked structure. Comparison of the selected excitations in 2P-1NH2-1 and 2P-1NH2-5 (Fig. S9) clearly showed that the type of stacking (i.e., the mutual orientation of the subunits within the bilayer conformer) affected different excitations in a qualitatively different way, which was also observed in case of other stacked-systems exhibiting fluorescence in the context of co-pigmentation [50].

On the contrary, all of the S1–S4 excitations in 2P-2NH2-1 occurred predominantly within the single subunit (Fig. S10), and followed similar patterns as those observed in 2P-1NH2-1; S0 →S1 and S0 →S3 were rather weak and red-shifted by about 10nm when compared to S0 →S1 (336nm, f =0.099) and S0 →S2 (318nm, f =0.185) in monolayer P-2NH2-1 (Fig. 6a), respectively. On the other hand, S0 →S2 and S0 →S4 in 2P-2NH2-1 were of a similar intensity as those of a subunit, and were red-shifted by only about 3nm comparing to S0 →S1 and S0 →S2 in P-2NH2-1, respectively. In 2P-2NH2-3, the lowest energy excitation intensities followed those of 2P-2NH2-1, with the exception of S0 →S1 that was red-shifted by about 20nm when compared to the respective excitation in monolayer. However, these low excitations span over the whole two-layered system.

It is worth noting that all of the considered 2P-1NH2 bilayers featured nearly forbidden or significantly weak S0 →S1 (f <0.01) and S0 →S2 transitions (f <0.04), which together with small energy differences among S1–S4 states affects the complexity of deexcitation processes possibly leading to fluorescence from higher excited states. On the other hand, the transition into S2 was of a higher intensity in conformers belonging to the 2P-2NH2 sample (f ∼ 0.1, except for the least favorable structure 2P-2NH2-4 with f =0.01), which could result in S2 fluorescence (Table S12). This finding indicates that the fluorescence of multilayer CDs may have different physical background (namely involvement of higher excited states) than the fluorescence of individual single layer CD components, despite the fact that they occupy the same emission band positions in fluorescence spectra. Unfortunately, this complicates the interpretation of emission spectra of the respective CDs and poses significant challenge for theoretical calculations, as extremely large number of conformers in excited states has to be taken into account to estimate reliable emission spectra of multilayered CDs.

For pyridinic nitrogen-doped two-layer systems, six 2P-1pd-N and four 2P-2pd-N conformers were identified by the search procedure (Tables S6, S7). Similar to amine models, the singly-doped structures did not differ significantly in energy, and the least stable structure 2P-1pd-N-6 was only 1.1kcal/mol higher in Gibbs free energy when compared to the most stable structure 2P-1pd-N-1. Similarly, 2P-2pd-N-3 and 2P-2pd-N-4 were about 1.1–1.7kcal/mol less favorable than the most stable structure within the class, 2P-2pd-N-1. Unlike 2P-1pd-N-1 (Table S5), in the most stable structure 2P-2pd-N-1 the subunits were graphite-like stacked, and nitrogen atoms were at the opposite sites of the system (Table S6). Most of the 2P-1pd-N and 2P-2pd-N conformers had a structure more or less significantly distorted from an ideal AB stacking.

Systems within the ensemble 2P-1pd-N had two absorption bands with maxima at about 217 and 306nm (Fig. 9c). Again, the high- and low-energy absorption of individual conformers lay in narrow intervals of 211–219 and 304–308nm, respectively. In 2P-2pd-N, the conformers’ absorption peaks at 209–216 and 298–302nm, and the overall sample absorption maxima were located at 214 and 301nm (Fig. 9d). When compared to the spectra of respective subunits P-1pd-N-1 (maxima located at 215 and 304nm) and P-2pd-N-1 (maxima at 213 and 298nm), the absorption did not change significantly. However, the spectral shapes of some two-layers such as 2P-1pd-N-4, 2P-2pd-N-1, 2P-2pd-N-2 and 2P-2pd-N-4, differed from the respective subunits P-1pd-N-1 and P-2pd-N-1, which spectra contained also shoulder peaks at ca. 245 and 240nm, respectively. The intensity and shape variation of spectra across the one- and two-layer structures were rather small.

Analysis of electronic density differences between the ground and respective excited states revealed that the lowest energy transitions S0 →S1 and S0 →S2 in the spectrum of 2P-1pd-N-1 spanned over the both layers (Fig. S11). However, the second transition had a more dominant contribution from one layer. The latter was also the case of excitations into S3 and S4. The only excitation (within the selected transitions) with a significant CT character was high-energy S0 →S25 (214nm, f =0.389), which fell within the absorption maximum band at 217nm. Localization of the respective transitions in 2P-1pd-N-6 (Fig. S12), the other 2P-1pd-N conformer chosen for a detail analysis of excitations, was similar to 2P-1pd-N-1. However, excitation into the lowest energy state S1 was significantly weaker in the latter system (f =0.002 comparing to f =0.089 in 2P-1pd-N-1), suggesting, together with a near and intense S0 →S2 transition (310nm, f =0.283, Table S12), possible S2 fluorescence.

Mutual orientation of subunits in 2P-2pd-N-1, the most stable structure within the sample 2P-2pd-N, resulted in the forbidden excitations into the lowest energy states S1 and S2 (Fig. S13). The lowest transition with f higher than 0.2 is S0 →S4 at 300nm, which had an evident contribution from the lone electron pairs located at the pyridinic nitrogen atoms in both layers. The latter electronic transition was related to S0 →S2 in P-2pd-N-1 with f =0.002 (Fig. 6b). Thus, stacking led to significant intensification of the excitation with a strong lone electron pair component in this particular case. As expected, different mutual position of subunits resulted in a different character of excitations, as observed also in the previous cases. Namely, transition into the state S1 in 2P-2pd-N-2 became allowed with f =0.056. Consequently, these alterations come to question when the fluorescence of the sample has to be estimated.

The last considered two-layer models were built from singly and doubly pyrrolic nitrogen-doped systems P-1pl-N-1 and P-2pl-N-1, respectively (Tables S8, S9). The Gibbs free energy differences between the most stable conformers and the least stable ones were about 2kcal/mol within the both ensembles. The ensemble absorption spectrum of the 2P-1pl-N conformers featured a low-energy band at 315nm, high-energy band at 224nm, and a middle-energy shoulder absorption at ca. 255nm (Fig. 9e). Similar to the previous systems, the absorption maxima of individual conformers lay in narrow wavelength intervals of 220–228 and 311–321nm, which agreed with the absorption maxima of P-1pl-N-1 located at 226 and 313nm, respectively. This suggests that the effects of the orientation of one sublayer with respect to the other one, and mutual interaction between pyrrolic nitrogen atoms were negligible in the overall absorption of the sample. The ensemble spectrum of 2P-2pl-N (Fig. 9f) features a low-energy band at 310nm (maxima of individual conformers are at 309–313nm), and a wide high-energy absorption at 235nm with a shoulder at 218nm (individual conformers: 234–241 and 205–218nm for main and shoulder peaks, respectively). While the low-energy (>300nm) absorption of P-2pl-N-1 (maximum at 309nm) was effectively the same as the sample absorption of the 2P-2pl-N, the absorption at 200–300nm was more intense in the bilayer.

Both S0 →S1 and S0 →S2 transitions in 2P-1pl-N-1 were mostly localized within a single layer (Fig. S14), and had moderate intensity (f values are 0.073 and 0.099 for S1 and S2, respectively). On the contrary, transitions into the lowest excited states S1 and S2 in 2P-1pl-N-3 (Fig. S15), the other chosen representative conformer within the 2P-1pl-N sample, span over the both layers and are effectively forbidden (f <0.005). Moreover, in all of the bilayer structures, the transition into S1 is weaker (f values in range 0.001–0.084) when compared to the subunit P-1pl-N-1 (f =0.145, Fig. 4c). Contrary to the above discussed amine and pyridinic nitrogen structures, none of the analyzed transitions could be clearly identified as an interlayer CT transition. Further, we opted for structure 2P-2pl-N-1 as a representative system within the 2P-2pl-N sample. The lowest energy transitions to S1–S4 states were localized within a single layer, and had f values in the range 0.01–0.17 (Fig. S16), which agrees with the lowest energy transition in the respective one-layered system P-2pl-N-1 (Fig. 6c), the building block of 2P-2pl-N. As was the case of 2P-1pl-N, the low-energy and intense electronic transitions do not possess a CT character. Finally, the S0 →S1 oscillator strength values were in the range 0.001–0.084 and 0.001–0.036 in all considered conformers belonging to 2P-1pl-N and 2P-2pl-N, respectively. Thus, while some of these structures would be prone to S1 fluorescence, the others could exhibit fluorescence from S2 or higher states. Therefore, individual conformers have to be treated individually to derive reliable ensemble fluorescence spectra of multilayered CDs, as already indicated for the models containing amine groups and pyridinic nitrogen.

We summarize our findings in Fig. 10 . The overall absorption spectra of the –NH2 substituted and pyrrolic-doped models (Fig. 10a) demonstrated a red-shift when compared to the spectrum of pyrene in both a vacuum and water (Fig. S17) that showed the low energy bands centered at 306 and 312nm in a vacuum and water, respectively. While electron donation effects arising from the presence of –NH2 groups in the respective models cause LUMO-HOMO gap narrowing, which points to the red-shifting of the low energy transitions with notable contributions from frontier (and near-frontier) orbitals, the extended aromatic systems of pyrrolic models could be viewed as the origin of the red-shift when compared to pristine pyrene. On the other hand, the low energy absorption of pyridinic models was nearly the same (P-1pd-N), or slightly blue-shifted (P-2pd-N), when compared to pyrene (Fig. 10a). The absorption spectra calculated in this study are in line with the experimentally measured spectra of various synthesized N-CDs, i.e. the calculated absorption maxima agree with the measured absorption at ∼200–250 and ∼270–350nm attributed to sp2 carbon domains and nitrogen centers (see, e.g., Refs. [13,51–54]), respectively. Contrary to absorption, the fluorescence spectra showed appreciable environment susceptibility, i.e. for all ensembles the emission in water was red-shifted with respect to the vacuum. Only the ensembles of structures containing –NH2 groups emitted at wavelengths above 400nm, which is close to previously reported fluorescence wavelengths of N-CDs in water (cf. Refs. [13,40–42,55,56]). This may indicate that the size and shape of the PAH that was used as a model for N-CD active centers is approaching the lower size limit for synthetized N-CDs.

Finally, the overall shapes of the 2L sample spectra were practically identical to the absorption spectra of the 1L structures used to build the respective 2L systems (Fig. 10b). In all of the 2L samples, the low-energy absorption of the 2L sample was red-shifted only by 1–6nm when compared to the corresponding 1L subunit. The high-energy bands were either slightly blue-shifted, such in the samples 2P-1NH2, 2P-2NH2 and 2P-1pl-N, or slightly red-shifted, like in 2P-1pd-N, 2P-2pd-N and 2P-2pl-N. Formation of 2L systems resulted also in slight elevation of the absorption intensity at about 200–300nm, but band height of the low-energy absorption above 300nm did not change significantly. On the other hand, a detailed analysis of the selected electronic transitions revealed that formation of the 2L systems modified the low-energy excitations in the way that could alter the overall emission behavior. Namely, the low-energy transitions occurred at near energy levels and, in many 2L conformers, the excitations into the lowest singlet excited states were effectively forbidden. Contrarily, excitations to some higher states became more probable. Therefore, unlike in 1L systems, emission from S2 (and higher excited states) could play a significant role in the deactivation processes of the 2L systems.

In this work, we have proposed a robust and systematic ensemble approach to model UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence features of N-CDs. The approach considered the various chemical forms of nitrogen that can be incorporated into the N-CD structure and took into account the abundance of individual isomers. It was possible to decipher the individual roles of various chemical forms of nitrogen, as well as their contributions to real spectra, which were constructed as a Boltzmann ensemble average. The amine pyrene models displayed appreciable red-shifted absorption and fluorescence in both vacuum and water environments with respect to the pyridinic and pyrrolic models. The red-shifting effects of amines and blue-shifting effects of pyridinic and pyrrolic nitrogen were more substantial when the nitrogen content of the N-CDs increased. It also implies that successful synthetic strategy leading to N-CDs with red emission should increase the presence of amine and reduce pyridinic and pyrrolic nitrogens in N-CD structures. The presented ensemble averaging approach was extended to models of the optical properties of multi-layer CDs, which are rich in rotational conformers, and can also be used during the design of doped CDs to screen the effects of structural motifs in relation to their optical characteristics. The analysis of the two-layered N-CD models indicated that the overall shape of the addressed optical properties was given by abundance of individual N-CD components and just slightly modulated by the exact arrangements/conformations of individual components in multilayered CDs. The overall picture of the two-layered N-CD spectra corresponded to the spectra of single-layer components, however, the detailed analysis showed that the stacking might affect absorption/emission involving higher excited states and contribute to the real emission properties of N-CDs. This finding calls for systematic and very detailed analysis of emission of multilayered CDs, which would be, however, very challenging and computationally demanding due to number and size of structures that had to be considered. Suitable strategy to address this challenge may benefit from a combination of robust conformational space sampling, which can be carried out by classical molecular dynamics, and multiscale quantum mechanics/molecular mechanics techniques.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Support from the Ministry of Education, Youth and Sports of the Czech Republic (Project No. LO1305) and the Czech Science Foundation (P208/12/G016) are gratefully acknowledged.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.plantsci.2004.08.011.
